elements.sent-2411-001: Scattering processes in the high-energy -- or Regge -- limit offer a rich laboratory to explore properties of gauge theories, both at amplitude and cross-section level.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this regime, the invariant mass of any pair of final-state particles grows with the scattering energy $\sqrt{s}$ and it is much larger than their individual transverse momenta that are instead held fixed.
elements.sent-2411-003: This can equivalently be expressed by requiring that the final-state particles are strongly ordered in rapidity while having comparable transverse momenta.
elements.sent-2411-004: Such a configuration for $2\to n$ scattering is referred to as multi-Regge kinematics (MRK).
elements.sent-2411-005: In this scenario, scattering amplitudes feature interesting properties, the most remarkable one being the phenomenon of reggeisation: they naturally organise in terms of $t$-channel exchanges of effective degrees of freedom called $reggeons$, whose propagator is dressed with a power-law behaviour $s^\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-006: In perturbative QCD, or more generally in non-abelian gauge theories, the dominant contribution is given by a reggeised gluon, whose power-law behaviour is controlled by the gluon Regge trajectory $\tau_g$.
elements.sent-2411-007: Following the seminal work by Fadin, Kuraev and Lipatov on the reggeisation of gauge bosons in non-abelian gauge theories with broken symmetries~\cite{Fadin:1975cb,Lipatov:1976zz , the investigation of QCD as a massless theory came shortly after~\cite{Kuraev:1976ge,Kuraev:1977fs,Balitsky:1978ic .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: These studies led to the conception of the celebrated Balitsky-Fadin-Kuraev-Lipatov (BFKL) formalism and its related evolution equation.
elements.sent-2411-009: The latter allows for the resummation of large terms of type $\ln (s/|t|)$ arising in the Regge limit at any order in perturbative QCD, both at leading-logarithmic (LL)~\cite{Fadin:1975cb,Lipatov:1976zz,Kuraev:1976ge,Kuraev:1977fs,Balitsky:1978ic , \ie $\left[\alpha_s \ln(s/|t|)\right]^n$, and next-to-leading-logarithmic (NLL) accuracy~\cite{Fadin:1998py,Ciafaloni:1998gs,Kotikov:2000pm , \ie $\alpha_s\left[\alpha_s \ln(s/|t|)\right]^n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Apart from its formal interest, the BFKL formalism has a broad spectrum of applications, ranging from the physics of small-$x$ parton distribution functions, see e.g. refs~\cite{Jaroszewicz:1982gr,Catani:1990eg,Altarelli:2001ji,Ciafaloni:2003ek , to the phenomenology of processes with large rapidity gaps in hadronic collisions, see e.g. refs~\cite{Mueller:1986ey,DelDuca:1993mn,Stirling:1994he,Andersen:2001kta,Colferai:2010wu,Ducloue:2013hia,Caporale:2014gpa,Andersen:2011hs,Andersen:2023kuj .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Improving the BFKL approach beyond the current frontier to reach next-to-next-to-leading-logarithmic (NNLL) accuracy, \ie $\alpha_s^2\left[\alpha_s \ln(s/|t|)\right]^n$, would significantly enhance those studies and our understanding of QCD in extreme regimes.
elements.sent-2411-012: The BFKL formalism relies on fundamental properties of scattering amplitudes in the high-energy regime.
elements.sent-2411-013: At LL accuracy, amplitudes are described to all-orders in perturbation theory as a tree-level exchange of reggeised gluons in the $t$-channel, whose power-law behaviour is fully determined by the one-loop Regge trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-014: In MRK, the interaction between these reggeons and the gluons emitted centrally in the large rapidity gap is described by an effective vertex known as the Lipatov or central-emission vertex (CEV)~\cite{Lipatov:1976zz .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Owing to single-reggeon exchanges in the $t$-channels, scattering amplitudes have a simple pole in the complex-angular momentum plane~\cite{Collins:1977jy,Ioffe:2010zz .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Thus, at LL accuracy, the iterated structure of reggeon propagators and CEVs goes under the name of Regge-pole factorisation.
elements.sent-2411-017: Starting at NLL, multi-reggeon exchanges appear and they give rise to cuts in the complex-angular momentum plane.
elements.sent-2411-018: However, these contribute solely to the absorptive (imaginary) part of the amplitude, whereas the dispersive (real) part is still described as a single reggeon exchange, thus showing a Regge-pole factorisation behaviour~\cite{Fadin:1998py .}
elements.sent-2411-019: At this logarithmic order, the factorised structure also entails the interaction between a single reggeon and the particles that sit at the edges of the large rapidity gap, defining the so-called impact factors.
elements.sent-2411-020: The latter are flavour-dependent, and in QCD, there are impact factors for both quarks and gluons.
elements.sent-2411-021: It is worth stressing that these are the only process-dependent ingredients in Regge-pole factorisation.
elements.sent-2411-022: Once they are accounted for, this factorisation reflects into a statement about Regge-pole universality in (M)RK.
elements.sent-2411-023: Several ingredients required for predictions in the BFKL formalism beyond LL accuracy are known.
elements.sent-2411-024: Two-loop corrections to the gluon Regge trajectory were computed long ago~\cite{Fadin:1996tb , and more recently three-loop ones in both $\mathcal{N =4$ super Yang-Mills (sYM)~\cite{Henn:2016jdu and full QCD~\cite{DelDuca:2021vjq,Caola:2021izf,Falcioni:2021dgr became available.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: One-loop QCD corrections to the quark and gluon impact factors were computed in refs.~\cite{Fadin:1992zt,Fadin:1993wh,Fadin:1993qb,Bern:1998sc,DelDuca:1998kx,Fadin:1999de and two-loop ones appeared in Ref.~\cite{Caron-Huot:2017fxr .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Finally, the CEV is known with one-loop accuracy in QCD~\cite{Fadin:1993wh,Fadin:1994fj,Fadin:1996yv,DelDuca:1998cx , and it was recently presented in dimensional regularisation up to second order in the regulator~\cite{Fadin:2023roz .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The only missing components required for computing the Regge-pole contribution to scattering amplitudes at NNLL are the one-loop corrections to the central two-gluon emission vertex, and the two-loop ones to the CEV.
elements.sent-2411-028: While the former has been recently presented in $\mathcal N=4$ sYM~\cite{Byrne:2022wzk , the latter is unknown.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Alongside the evaluation of these contributions, an outstanding issue in QCD that prevents a robust generalisation of the BFKL framework beyond NLL is the appearance of cuts in the complex angular momentum plane.
elements.sent-2411-030: These are understood as multi-reggeon exchanges.
elements.sent-2411-031: Starting from NNLL, such cuts also appear in the dispersive part of the result, making the identification of the Regge pole contribution problematic.\footnote{This scenario is very different in planar $\mathcal N=4$ sYM, leading to a much better understanding, see e.g. section 6 in Ref.~\cite{Caron-Huot:2013fea and references therein.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: High-energy factorisation breaking at $\NNLL$ in the real part of two-loop $2\to2$ QCD scattering amplitudes was first reported in Ref.~\cite{DelDuca:2001gu .}
elements.sent-2411-033: The observation that factorisation-violating terms are infrared (IR) divergent motivated investigations into their contributions to the IR poles of scattering amplitudes at two- and three-loop orders in QCD~\cite{DelDuca:2013ara,DelDuca:2014cya .}
elements.sent-2411-034: In the recent past, several approaches have appeared to address this problem in a systematic way.
elements.sent-2411-035: Ref.~\cite{Caron-Huot:2013fea developed an effective theory based on the Balitsky/JIMWLK formalism~\cite{Balitsky:1995ub,Jalilian-Marian:1997qno,Jalilian-Marian:1997jhx,Jalilian-Marian:1997ubg,Kovner:2000pt,Weigert:2000gi,Iancu:2000hn,Iancu:2001ad,Ferreiro:2001qy,Caron-Huot:2013fea that paved the way for many amplitude-level investigations, see e.g.~\cite{Caron-Huot:2017fxr,Caron-Huot:2020grv,Falcioni:2020lvv,Falcioni:2021buo,Falcioni:2021dgr .}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Fadin and Lipatov studied instead the complete contribution of three-reggeon cuts to the $2\to 2$ scattering amplitude, using a diagrammatic approach~\cite{Fadin:2017nka,Fadin:2019tdt,Fadin:2021csi,Fadin:2024eyf .}
elements.sent-2411-037: More recently, a SCET-based formalism based on Glauber exchanges has also been developed~\cite{Rothstein:2016bsq,Rothstein:2024fpx,Moult:2022lfy,Gao:2024qsg .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Thanks to impressive progress on the calculation of multi-loop multi-leg scattering amplitudes~\cite{Abreu:2018aqd,Caron-Huot:2020vlo,Caola:2021izf,Caola:2022dfa,Agarwal:2023suw,DeLaurentis:2023izi,DeLaurentis:2023nss , we now have analytic data that allow us to $a)$ validate the approaches described above, $b)$ gain direct insight into the high-energy structure of perturbative QCD, $c)$ extract the universal building blocks required to extend the BFKL programme beyond NLL accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-039: In this paper, we take an important step in this direction by considering the high-energy limit of the $2\to 3$ QCD scattering amplitudes in full colour and kinematics~\cite{Agarwal:2023suw,DeLaurentis:2023izi,DeLaurentis:2023nss and comparing them against predictions that we obtained from the framework of Ref.~\cite{Caron-Huot:2013fea .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: By matching against the EFT~\cite{Caron-Huot:2013fea , we both validate this approach at NNLL in MRK at the two-loop level, and extract for the first time the universal two-loop vertex that describes the emission of a central-rapidity gluon.}
elements.sent-2411-041: This can be seen as a crucial step towards a robust definition of the Lipatov vertex at two loops and beyond.
elements.sent-2411-042: The remainder of this paper is organised as follows: in \cref{se:generalaspects we discuss the $2 \to 3$ MRK, list the contributing partonic channels, describe aspects related to signature and colour and illustrate how we expanded the five-point QCD scattering amplitudes~\cite{Agarwal:2023suw in MRK.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Section \ref{se:shockwave is devoted to a review of the Balitsky/JIMWLK formalism and to a discussion of how it can be used to predict the form of our scattering amplitudes in MRK.}
elements.sent-2411-044: We do this by extending the results of Ref.~\cite{Caron-Huot:2013fea and expressing the two-loop scattering amplitude in terms of fully-predicted quantities and one unknown two-loop universal vertex that describes the emission of the central-rapidity gluon.}
elements.sent-2411-045: In \cref{se:results we compare the results from \cref{se:generalaspects,se:shockwave .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: After finding full agreement between the two for all the terms that we predict unambiguously at NNLL, we leverage the knowledge of the full $2 \to 3$ QCD amplitude to extract for the first time the two-loop universal vertex, both in QCD and $\mathcal{N =4$ sYM.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Moreover, we exploit the well-known IR structure of two-loop gauge-theory amplitudes to define finite remainders for their universal building blocks in the high-energy regime.
elements.sent-2411-048: We also document the various checks that we have performed to validate our calculations.
elements.sent-2411-049: We present our conclusions, final remarks and outlook in \cref{se:conclusions .}
elements.sent-2411-050: In this section we discuss the defining features of five-point scattering amplitudes in MRK.
elements.sent-2411-051: We begin with a precise description of the kinematics, and then turn to the discussion of signature eigenstates as well as the choice of appropriate colour bases for the various partonic channels.
elements.sent-2411-052: In the second half we describe how we obtain the high-energy limit of $2\to3$ amplitudes up to two loops starting from their known expressions in general kinematics~\cite{Agarwal:2023suw , with an emphasis on the expansion of the transcendental functions.}
elements.sent-2411-053: We conclude by presenting our results for the infrared-subtracted scattering amplitudes.

elements.sent-2411-221: In this section, we will briefly summarise how one can obtain predictions for scattering amplitudes in MRK from the Balitsky/JIMWLK formalism~\cite{Balitsky:1995ub,Jalilian-Marian:1997qno,Jalilian-Marian:1997jhx,Jalilian-Marian:1997ubg,Kovner:2000pt,Weigert:2000gi,Iancu:2000hn,Iancu:2001ad,Ferreiro:2001qy,Caron-Huot:2013fea .}
elements.sent-2411-222: Although nothing presented in this section is conceptually new, to the best of our knowledge many of the required details have not been spelled out explicitly before, so we report them here.
elements.sent-2411-223: We start from a brief summary of the formalism itself, but we refer the reader to the vast literature on the subject, and in particular to Ref.~\cite{Caron-Huot:2013fea , for a more in-depth presentation.}
elements.sent-2411-224: Before moving on, we take the opportunity to introduce the graphical notation and some nomenclature that we will use in this section.
elements.sent-2411-225: As already explained, the process involves two highly boosted ``projectiles" which retain their identities in the interaction.
elements.sent-2411-226: We will refer to these as $A$ and $B$ lines, in accordance with the notation of \cref{eq:partchann .}
elements.sent-2411-227: We will also use the $A/B$ subscripts to denote quantities associated to the respective lines.
elements.sent-2411-228: As depicted in \cref{fig:MRK_notation , we will use blue double lines to draw projectile $A$ and red double lines to draw projectile $B$.}
elements.sent-2411-229: The double lines can indicate either gluons or (anti-)quarks.
elements.sent-2411-230: In \cref{fig:MRK_notation the dashed blob represents the full interaction among the projectiles $A$ and $B$ and with the central-rapidity gluon.}

elements.subsection-2411-005: The MRK amplitude from the Balitsky/JIMWLK formalism
elements.sent-2411-231: The Balitsky/JIMWLK formalism is a convenient framework to analyse systems with large rapidity gaps.
elements.sent-2411-232: In this formalism, in the strict high-energy limit ($x=0$), the line $A$ is represented by a single infinite Wilson line $U^{\{r\}}$, which in position space is localised on the $x_+=0$ lightcone and only depends non trivially on transverse coordinates $\mathbf z$: \begin{equation}U^{\{r\}}(z) = \mathcal P e^{ig_s\int_{-\infty}^\infty dx^- A^a_- (x^+=0,\, x^-, \,\mathbf{z}) T_r^{a}},\end{equation} where $A$ is the gluon field, $\mathcal P$ is the usual path-ordering in colour space, and $r$ stands for the colour representation.\footnote{In what follows, we will omit $r$ whenever this does not create ambiguities.}
elements.formula-2411-055: \begin{equation}U^{\{r\}}(z) = \mathcal P e^{ig_s\int_{-\infty}^\infty dx^- A^a_- (x^+=0,\, x^-, \,z) T_r^{a}},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-233: Such infinite Wilson lines are divergent, and can be regulated by working at finite rapidity.
elements.sent-2411-234: Their evolution in rapidity -- which generates the large $\ln x$ logs -- is also known, and at LO is given by the celebrated Balitsky/JIMWLK equation.
elements.sent-2411-235: The latter is a non-linear equation and, in particular, the evolution in rapidity generates additional Wilson lines.
elements.sent-2411-236: At finite rapidity, line $A$ can be represented as \begin{equation}\label{eq:projectile_expansion_wilson} \begin{aligned} a_{\lambda_1}^{a_1,\dagger}(p_1) a_{\lambda_5}^{a_5}(p_5) \sim 2\pi \delta(p_1^+ - p_5^+) \delta_{\lambda_1\lambda_5} \times 2 p_1^+ \times \Big[ \IFpre(\qp_A) \overline{U}_{\eta_A}(\qp_A) + \frac{1}{2}\int \measF{\qp} \, \IFpre'(\qp_A,\qp) \overline{U}_{\eta_A}(\qp_A-\qp)\overline{U}_{\eta_A}(\qp) + \dots \Big]^{a_1 a_5} , \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $a_{\lambda_1 ^{a_1,\dagger (p_1)$ and $ a_{\lambda_5 ^{a_5 (p_5) $ are creation and annihilation operators of external states.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-237: Moreover, in \cref{eq:projectile_expansion_wilson , $2 p_1^+$ is the standard eikonal vertex (with our definition of the lightcone coordinates), the subscript $\eta_A$ indicates that we have regulated the Wilson line by tilting it by a fixed rapidity $\eta_A = 1/2 \ln p_5^+/p_5^-$, and we have defined \begin{equation}\label{eq:fourierdef} \overline{U}_\eta(\pp)\equiv U_\eta(\pp)-\mathbb{I} \quad \text{and} \quad U_\eta(\pp) = \int \meas{\zp} \, e^{-i \qp \cdot \zp} U_\eta(\zp) .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-239: The coefficients $\IFpre$ and $\IFpre'$ in \cref{eq:projectile_expansion_wilson are radiatively-generated impact factors.}
elements.sent-2411-240: We note that $\IFpre'$ is a colour operator so that the product $\IFpre' \overline{U}\, \overline{U}$ is in the same representation of the $A$ projectile.
elements.sent-2411-241: In our normalisation, $\IFpre \sim 1 + \mathcal O(\as)$ and $\IFpre' \sim \mathcal O(\as)$.

elements.sent-2411-263: At LO the $W$ fields are free, so that their same-rapidity correlator is \begin{equation}\label{eq:vev_1} \begin{aligned} \Big\langle \timeOrd \big[W(\pp_1) \cdots W(\pp_n)\big]_\eta \big[\Wb(\qp_1) \cdots \Wb(\qp_m)\big]_\eta \Big\rangle = \delta_{nm}\sum_{\sigma \in S_n} G(\pp_1,\qp_{\sigma(1)}) \dots G(\pp_n,\qp_{\sigma(n)}) + \mathcal{O}(\as) , \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\timeOrd$ is the standard time ordering, $\Wb$ is associated with projectile $B$, and the 2-point correlator reads \begin{equation}\label{eq:vev_0} G(\pp,\qp) = \Big\langle \timeOrd\, W^a_\eta(\pp) \Wb^b_\eta(\qp) \Big\rangle = (2 \pi)^{2-2\epsilon} \delta^{2-2\epsilon}(\pp-\qp) \frac{i \delta^{ab}}{\pp^2} + \mathcal{O}(\as).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-264: Due to CPT invariance, the correlator between even and odd numbers of $W$ fields is zero at any order ($W$ is a signature-odd field)~\cite{Caron-Huot:2013fea .}
elements.sent-2411-265: Beyond LO, it is convenient to redefine the $W$ field \begin{equation}W \to (1+\alpha_s r_1 + \dots )W + (\alpha_s^2 s_1 + \dots )WWW + \dots ,\end{equation} such that $a)$ correlators with different numbers of $W$ fields on the two lightcones vanish, \begin{equation}\label{eq:vev_2} \Big\langle \timeOrd \big[W(\pp_1) \cdots W(\pp_n)\big]_\eta \big[\Wb(\qp_1) \cdots \Wb(\qp_m)\big]_\eta \Big\rangle = 0 \quad \quad \text{for} \quad n \neq m,\end{equation} and $b)$ the two-point correlator is equal to the free one \begin{equation}\label{eq:vev_3} G(\pp,\qp) = \Big\langle \timeOrd\, W^a_\eta(\pp) \Wb^b_\eta(\qp) \Big\rangle = (2 \pi)^{2-2\epsilon} \delta^{2-2\epsilon}(\pp-\qp) \frac{i \delta^{ab}}{\pp^2},\end{equation} at any order in $\as$.

elements.subsection-2411-007: NLL generalisation to $2\to n$ scattering and evolution redefinition
elements.sent-2411-330: Starting from NLL, we find it convenient to slightly redefine the evolution variable $\Delta\eta_{ij}$, the impact factors $\IFpre$, and the vertex correction $\wilsonV_\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-331: Such a redefinition is immaterial for the final result, but it allows us to make contact with the Regge formalism.
elements.sent-2411-332: To explain how this comes about, we first note that also at NLL the $t$-channel structure of \cref{eq:myNLL iterates, for the same reasons discussed in the previous subsection.}
elements.figure-2411-007: \subfigure{0.9{\includegraphics[valign=m]{tikz-fig/fig_22.pdf}} \label{fig:TWOtoN}} \subfigure{1{\includegraphics[valign=m]{tikz-fig/fig_23.pdf}} \label{fig:vertexDef}} \caption{Schematic depiction of $2\to n$ MRK odd signature scattering amplitudes. \label{fig:regge_pole_2ton}}
elements.sent-2411-333: Schematically, the signature-odd $2\to n$ NLL amplitude reads (see \cref{fig:regge_pole_2ton ) \begin{equation}\begin{split} \label{eq:myNLLn} \mathcal A^{(--)}_{\NLL} \propto \quad (T_{r_A})_{c_n c_1}^{a_{n-1}} \IFpre_A(\qp_{n-1}) \times \frac{e^{\tau_{n-1}\Delta\eta_{n,n-1}}}{\qp_{n-1}^2} \mathcal V_{n-1} \frac{e^{\tau_{n-2}\Delta\eta_{n-1,n-2}}}{\qp_{n-2}^2}\cdots \frac{e^{\tau_{4}\Delta\eta_{5,4}}}{\qp_{4}^2} \mathcal V_{4} \frac{e^{\tau_{3}\Delta\eta_{4,3}}}{\qp_{3}^2} \times \IFpre_B(\qp_3) (T_{r_B})_{c_3 c_2}^{a_3} , \end{split}\end{equation} where we have omitted irrelevant overall prefactors and used the shorthands \begin{equation}\label{eq:short_tau_V} \tau_i = \tau_g(\qp_i), \quad\quad \mathcal V_i = i f^{a_{j_i} a_{j_{i-1}} a_i} V_{\lambda_i} (\qp_{i},\pp_i) \wilsonV_{\lambda_i} (\qp_{i},\pp_i).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-106: \begin{equation}\label{eq:short_tau_V} \tau_i = \tau_g(\qp_i), \quad\quad \mathcal V_i = i f^{a_{j_i} a_{j_{i-1}} a_i} V_{\lambda_i} (\qp_{i},\pp_i) \wilsonV_{\lambda_i} (\qp_{i},\pp_i).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-334: In order to improve readability, from this point onwards we will drop the dependence on the two-dimensional momenta from the impact factors and the vertex correction.
elements.sent-2411-335: This implicit dependence can be easily and unambiguously inferred from their indices.
elements.sent-2411-336: We now write the rapidity differences as \begin{equation}\label{eq:fromETAtoL} \begin{split} \Delta\eta_{ij} = \ln\frac{|s_{ij}|}{|\pp_i||\pp_j|} = \ln\frac{|s_{ij}|}{\mu^2_{ij}} + \frac{1}{2} \ln\frac{\mu_{ij}^2}{\pp_i^2} + \frac{1}{2} \ln\frac{\mu_{ij}^2}{\pp_j^2} = = L_{ij} + \frac{1}{2} \left(\ln\frac{\mu_{ij}^2}{\pp_i^2} + \frac{i\pi}{2}\right) + \frac{1}{2} \left(\ln\frac{\mu_{ij}^2}{\pp_j^2} + \frac{i\pi}{2}\right), \end{split}\end{equation} where $\mu_{ij}$ is a $\mathcal O(1)$ scale and where we have introduced the signature-even logarithm \begin{equation}L_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\ln\frac{s_{ij}}{\mu^2_{ij}} + \ln\frac{-s_{ij}}{\mu^2_{ij}}\right)= \ln\frac{|s_{ij}|}{\mu^2_{ij}} - \frac{i\pi}{2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-337: Our goal is to redefine all quantities in \cref{eq:myNLLn so that the rapidity evolution is expressed in terms of exponentials of the type $e^{\tau_{i L_{i+1,i }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-338: The leftover terms in \cref{eq:fromETAtoL then need to be reabsorbed in $\IFpre$ and $\wilsonV_\lambda$.}
elements.sent-2411-339: In practice we redefine the impact factors and vertex corrections as \begin{equation}\label{eq:redefinition_nll} \IFpre_A\, e^{\frac{\tau_{n-1}}{2}\left( \ln\frac{\mu_{n,n-1}^2}{\pp_n^2} + \frac{i\pi}{2}\right)} \cos^{-\frac{1}{2}}\left(\frac{\pi\tau_{n-1}}{2} \right) \equiv \Bar{\IFpre}_A, \notag \IFpre_B\, e^{\frac{\tau_{3}}{2}\left( \ln\frac{\mu_{4,3}^2}{\pp_3^2} + \frac{i\pi}{2}\right)} \cos^{-\frac{1}{2}}\left(\frac{\pi \tau_{3}}{2} \right) \equiv \Bar{\IFpre}_B, \cos^{\frac{1}{2}}\left( \frac{\pi \tau_{i}}{2}\right) e^{\frac{\tau_{i}}{2} \left(\ln\frac{\mu^2_{i+1,i}}{\pp_i^2} + \frac{i\pi}{2}\right)} \wilsonV_{\lambda_i} e^{\frac{\tau_{i-1}}{2}\left(\ln\frac{\mu^2_{i,i-1}}{\pp_{i}^2} + \frac{i\pi}{2}\right)} \cos^{-\frac{1}{2}}\left( \frac{\pi \tau_{i-1}}{2}\right) \equiv \overline {\wilsonV}_{\lambda_i}, \notag\end{equation} which also prompts us to introduce $\overline { \mathcal V _i = i f^{a_{j_i a_{j_{i-1 a_i V_{\lambda_i \overline{\wilsonV _{\lambda_i}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-340: The redefinition above introduces a dependence on the arbitrary factorisation scales $\mu_{ij}$ in the new quantities $\Bar{\IFpre}$ and $\overline{\wilsonV}$.
elements.sent-2411-341: In particular they satisfy the RGE-like equations \begin{equation}\label{eq:RGE_mu} \Bar{\IFpre}(\mu') = \Bar{\IFpre}(\mu) \left(\frac{\mu'}{\mu}\right)^{\tau_i}, \quad\quad \overline{\wilsonV}_{\lambda_i}(\mu'_1,\mu'_2) = \left(\frac{\mu_1'}{\mu_1}\right)^{\tau_{i-1}} \overline{\wilsonV}_{\lambda_i}(\mu_1,\mu_2) \left(\frac{\mu_2'}{\mu_2}\right)^{\tau_i}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-342: For simplicity, we set all scales $\mu_{ij = \rho$ and keep $\rho$ implicit.}
elements.sent-2411-343: If needed, one can easily reconstruct the full scale dependence using \cref{eq:RGE_mu .}
elements.sent-2411-344: We also point out that the cosine factors in \cref{eq:redefinition_nll are immaterial at $\NLL$ and can be replaced by $1$ at this order.}

elements.sent-2411-346: The NLL result \cref{eq:myNLLn then becomes \begin{equation}\mathcal A_{\NLL} \propto (T_{r_A})_{c_n c_1}^{a_{n-1}}\Bar{\IFpre}_A \frac{e^{\tau_{n-1}L_{n,n-1}}}{\qp_{n-1}^2} \overline{\mathcal V}_{n-1} \frac{e^{\tau_{n-2}L_{n-1,n-2}}}{\qp_{n-2}^2}\cdots \frac{e^{\tau_{4}L_{5,4}}}{\qp_{4}^2} \overline{\mathcal V}_{4} \frac{e^{\tau_{3}L_{4,3}}}{\qp_{3}^2} \Bar{\IFpre}_B (T_{r_B})_{c_3 c_2}^{a_3} . \label{eq:myNLLnNEW}\end{equation} We conclude this section by presenting explicit results for the $2\to2$ and $2\to 3$ amplitudes.}

elements.sent-2411-348: The amplitudes read \begin{equation}\mathcal A_{2\to 2,\NLL}^{(--)} = \Bar{\IFpre}_A e^{\tau_s L_{s} } \cos \left( \frac{\pi \tau_s}{2}\right) \Bar{\IFpre}_B\times \mathcal A_{2\to 2}^{(0)}, \label{eq:2to2_factorised} A^{(--)}_{2\to 3,\NLL} = \Bar{\IFpre}_A e^{\tau_A L_A } \overline{\wilsonV}_{\lambda_4} e^{\tau_B L_B } \Bar{\IFpre}_B \times \mathcal A^{(0)}_{2\to 3}, \label{eq:2to3_factorised}\end{equation} where for the $2\to 2$ case we have defined $\qp = q_3$, $\tau_s = \tau_3$, $L_s = L_{34}$, and for the $2\to 3$ case \begin{equation}L_A = L_{45}, \quad L_B = L_{34}, \quad \qp_A = \qp_4, \quad \qp_B = \qp_3, \quad \tau_A = \tau_4, \quad \tau_B = \tau_3,\end{equation} in the notation of \cref{fig:regge_pole_2ton .}

elements.sent-2411-508: In this paper, we have presented an analysis of two-loop $2 \to 3$ QCD scattering amplitudes in the high-energy limit.
elements.sent-2411-509: To do so, we extended the results of Ref.~\cite{Caron-Huot:2013fea to deal with all the contributions required for predicting the signature-odd two-loop $2\to3$ MRK amplitude, and compared against a direct expansion of full-colour QCD amplitudes.}
elements.sent-2411-510: This was made possible thanks to the recent publication of the latter~\cite{Agarwal:2023suw,DeLaurentis:2023izi,DeLaurentis:2023nss .}
elements.sent-2411-511: Our calculation allowed us to test MRK universality at the NNLL level for the first time.
elements.sent-2411-512: We did so by separating contributions which are independent of the flavour of the partons participating in the scattering, from others which are flavour dependent but that can still be described in terms of universal functions.
elements.sent-2411-513: While doing so, we were also able to test predictions for quantities which are closely related to Regge cuts.
elements.sent-2411-514: We found that, besides the well-understood flavour dependence of the impact factors, the only source of non-universality arises from subleading-colour terms, which are accurately captured by multiple exchanges of the $W$ field of \cref{se:shockwave .}
elements.sent-2411-515: These can in turn be expressed in terms of single-valued, pure, and uniform-transcendental quantities describing the kinematics in the transverse plane.
elements.sent-2411-516: The form of these terms is also universal, but it involves colour operators which act on the the tree-level, and hence depend on the colour representation of the scattering particles.
elements.sent-2411-517: As an important product of our investigations, we were able to extract for the first time the vertex describing the emission of a central-rapidity gluon, both in QCD and $\mathcal N=4$ sYM, at two-loop accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-518: We verified that the latter is given by the leading-transcendental contribution of the former.
elements.sent-2411-519: This quantity, closely related to the Lipatov vertex, was the last missing ingredient required to calculate NNLL signature-odd amplitudes in MRK, to any multiplicity.
elements.sent-2411-520: Indeed, once higher-multiplicity two-loop amplitudes will become available in the literature, our results could provide a strong check of them.
elements.sent-2411-521: We provide our main results through the ancillary files published alongside this paper.
elements.sent-2411-522: Our study opens several interesting avenues for further investigation.
elements.sent-2411-523: In this paper, we have limited ourselves to the study of signature-even amplitudes at the one-loop level only.
elements.sent-2411-524: It would be interesting to push this to higher orders.
elements.sent-2411-525: It would be equally interesting to relax the strong rapidity ordering requirement and study the next-to-MRK limit, and to explore the connections between our results and the ongoing analysis within the SCET framework~\cite{Rothstein:2016bsq,Rothstein:2024fpx,Moult:2022lfy,Gao:2024qsg .}
elements.sent-2411-526: Also, we observed intriguing connections between the Regge and IR structures of the theory.
elements.sent-2411-527: In the past, studying these connections served as a guide towards a better understanding of both.
elements.sent-2411-528: We anticipate that investigations in this direction may help elucidate the relation between the quantities described in this paper and the ones arising in the standard BFKL literature.
elements.sent-2411-529: This could potentially pave the way towards a better understanding of the structure of Regge poles and Regge cuts in QCD, along the lines reviewed in Ref.~\cite{Fadin:2024eyf .}
elements.sent-2411-530: This would put on a solid ground the precise relation between universal objects appearing in the framework~\cite{Caron-Huot:2013fea that we used in this paper, and analogous ones in QCD Regge theory, like the Lipatov vertex.}
elements.sent-2411-531: Although it is natural to imagine a close connection between the two along the lines of Ref.~\cite{Falcioni:2021dgr , a rigorous proof of their precise relation is still lacking.}
elements.sent-2411-532: Establishing this could require studying the evolution and the structure of radiative corrections of the quantities described in \cref{se:shockwave,se:results .}
elements.sent-2411-533: This would in turn be crucial for a generalisation of BFKL theory in QCD beyond NLL.
elements.sent-2411-534: We look forward to pursuing these research avenues in the future.

elements.sent-2411-571: To find explicit representations for the polarisation vectors, we use the standard spinor-helicity representation\footnote{Compared to standard spinor-helicity literature, here we use $\vep^{*}$ (rather than the short-hand $\vep$) to make it explicit the fact that the gluon is out-going. \begin{equation}\vep_+^{*\mu} \equiv \frac{1}{\sqrt2}\frac{\langle q \gamma^\mu p_4]}{\langle q p_4\rangle}, \quad\quad \vep_-^{*\mu} \equiv -\frac{1}{\sqrt2}\frac{[ q \gamma^\mu p_4 \rangle}{[q p_4]},\end{equation} with $q=p_2$ for $\vep^{* _{\lambda_4}$ and $q=p_1$ for $\tilde\vep^{* _{\lambda_4}$.. Explicitly \begin{equation}\label{eq:explicit_polvec} \vep^*_+ = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\frac{\pPc{4}}{p^+_4},1,-i,-\frac{\pPc{4}}{p^+_4}\right), \quad \vep^*_- = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\frac{\pP{4}}{p^+_4},1,i,-\frac{\pP{4}}{p^+_4}\right), \notag \tilde\vep^*_+ = \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\frac{p^+_4}{\pP{4}},\frac{\pPc{4}}{\pP{4}},i \frac{\pPc{4}}{\pP{4}}, \frac{p^+_4}{\pP{4}}\right), \quad \tilde\vep^*_- = \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\frac{p^+_4}{\pPc{4}},\frac{\pP{4}}{\pPc{4}},-i \frac{\pP{4}}{\pPc{4}}, \frac{p^+_4}{\pPc{4}}\right).\end{equation}.}}}

elements.sent-2411-576: The $\beta$-function coefficients used in this paper are defined via \begin{equation}\frac{d \as }{d \ln \mu} = \beta(\as) - 2\epsilon \as \; , \quad \beta(\as) = -2 \as \sum\limits_{n=0} \beta_n \left(\frac{\as}{4 \pi}\right)^{n+1}\end{equation} so that up to two-loops we have \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \beta_0 = \frac{11}{3} C_A - \frac{2}{3}\: N_f , \beta_1 = \frac{34}{3} \: C_A^2-\frac{10}{3}\: C_A \:N_f - 2 \:C_F\: N_f , \end{aligned}\end{equation} with the $SU(N_c)$ Casimir constants \begin{equation}C_A = N_c, \quad C_F = \frac{N_c^2-1}{2N_c} .\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-596: Schematically, the signature-odd $n$-point NLL amplitude reads (see \cref{fig:regge_pole_2ton ) \begin{equation}\begin{split} \label{eq:myNLLn} \mathcal A^{(--)}_{\NLL} \propto \quad (T_{r_A})_{c_n c_1}^{a_{n-1}} \IFpre_A(\qp_{n-1}) \times \frac{e^{\tau_{n-1}\Delta\eta_{n,n-1}}}{\qp_{n-1}^2} \mathcal V_{n-1} \frac{e^{\tau_{n-2}\Delta\eta_{n-1,n-2}}}{\qp_{n-2}^2}\cdots \frac{e^{\tau_{4}\Delta\eta_{5,4}}}{\qp_{4}^2} \mathcal V_{4} \frac{e^{\tau_{3}\Delta\eta_{4,3}}}{\qp_{3}^2} \times \IFpre_B(\qp_3) (T_{r_B})_{c_3 c_2}^{a_3} , \end{split}\end{equation} where we have omitted irrelevant overall prefactors and used the shorthands \begin{equation}\label{eq:short_tau_V} \tau_i = \tau_g(\qp_i), \quad\quad \mathcal V_i = i f^{a_{j_i} a_{j_{i-1}} a_i} V_{\lambda_i} (\qp_{i},\pp_i) \wilsonV_{\lambda_i} (\qp_{i},\pp_i).\end{equation}.}
